coredata.dc:title: Dual-response fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots and europium ions hybrid for ratiometric and on-site visual determination of oxytetracycline and tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Huifang
Chen, Yubing
Xu, Mengqi
Ling, Yuwei
Ju, Shiying
Tang, Yanfeng
Tong, Changlun
coredata.dc:description: Tetracyclines residues, particularly oxytetracycline (OTC) and tetracycline (TC), have raised extensive concern because of their serious adverse effects on human health. Herein, a dual-response fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Eu3+ hybrid (N-CDs–Eu3+) was developed to selectively determine OTC and TC. The N-CDs act as ancillary ligands of Eu3+ and recognition units of OTC/TC, while the Eu3+ ions chelated with N-CDs can also specifically recognize OTC/TC. Upon inclusion of OTC/TC, an enhancement in Eu3+ emission occurs due to the energy transfer from OTC/TC to Eu3+ and the efficient elimination of quenching effect caused by H2O molecule, which is attributed to the incorporation of N-CDs; while the blue fluorescence emitted by the N-CDs decreases under the inner filter effect and static quenching effect caused by OTC/TC. Based on the double and reverse response signals, the ratiometric detection of OTC and TC in the range of 0.1–45 μΜ and 0.1–30 μΜ is achieved with a detection limit of 0.017 and 0.041 μM, respectively. In addition, the noticeable variation in fluorescence color of the probe is integrated with a smartphone-assisted analysis device for the rapid on-site quantitative assay of OTC, where the detection limit is 0.15 μΜ. The results show that this probe performs with excellent specificity and anti-interference for both OTC and TC, and satisfactory detection results are obtained in lake water, milk, and honey samples, thereby confirming that the probe exhibits promising application in food safety and environmental monitoring.

head.ce:title: Dual-response fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots and europium ions hybrid for ratiometric and on-site visual determination of oxytetracycline and tetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracyclines residues, particularly oxytetracycline (OTC) and tetracycline (TC), have raised extensive concern because of their serious adverse effects on human health. Herein, a dual-response fluorescent probe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Eu3+ hybrid (N-CDs–Eu3+) was developed to selectively determine OTC and TC. The N-CDs act as ancillary ligands of Eu3+ and recognition units of OTC/TC, while the Eu3+ ions chelated with N-CDs can also specifically recognize OTC/TC. Upon inclusion of OTC/TC, an enhancement in Eu3+ emission occurs due to the energy transfer from OTC/TC to Eu3+ and the efficient elimination of quenching effect caused by H2O molecule, which is attributed to the incorporation of N-CDs; while the blue fluorescence emitted by the N-CDs decreases under the inner filter effect and static quenching effect caused by OTC/TC. Based on the double and reverse response signals, the ratiometric detection of OTC and TC in the range of 0.1–45 μΜ and 0.1–30 μΜ is achieved with a detection limit of 0.017 and 0.041 μM, respectively. In addition, the noticeable variation in fluorescence color of the probe is integrated with a smartphone-assisted analysis device for the rapid on-site quantitative assay of OTC, where the detection limit is 0.15 μΜ. The results show that this probe performs with excellent specificity and anti-interference for both OTC and TC, and satisfactory detection results are obtained in lake water, milk, and honey samples, thereby confirming that the probe exhibits promising application in food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines comprise a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that possess excellent antibacterial properties and exhibit therapeutic effects by inhibiting the synthesis of various bacterial proteins (Chopra and Roberts, 2001). In particular, oxytetracycline (OTC) and tetracycline (TC) are extensively applied for preventing bacterial infections in aquaculture and animal husbandry owing to their hypotoxicity, inexpensiveness, and good oral absorption (Naik et al., 2017; Zhi et al., 2020). Unfortunately, the long-term use and acute overdose of tetracyclines have resulted in the pollution of their large residues in environmental water sources and animal-derived foods (e.g., milk, meat, and honey) because of their extremely stability, which pose a severe potential burden to human health as they can cause liver damage, gastrointestinal diseases, allergic reactions, and antibiotic resistance (Gajda et al., 2013; Oliver et al., 2011; Phillips et al., 2004). To prevent such adverse effects on consumers, allowable residue limits (MRL) have been set for tetracyclines in various animal products. Numerous systematic approaches have been developed to detect tetracyclines residues, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (Perez-Silva et al., 2012), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Gajda et al., 2017), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Widiastuti and Anastasia, 2015), capillary electrophoresis (Casado-Terrones et al., 2007), and electrochemical analysis (Peng et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2016). These assay technologies can determine the tetracyclines with high sensitivity and specificity, while most of them suffer from the drawbacks of being expensive to conduct, requiring sophisticated instrument to perform, involving complicated sample preparation steps, and being time-consuming (Li et al., 2020a). Therefore, there is an urgent need to establish new approaches for tetracyclines residues determination that feature easy operation, high sensitivity, and excellent specificity.

Fluorescence spectroscopy-based analysis techniques possess excellent advantages such as a fast response, easy operation, visualization, high sensitivity and cost-effectiveness, which is a preferred alternative for determining certain targets in food samples (Jia et al., 2021; Wu and Tong, 2018). In recent years, lanthanide-based luminescent materials have aroused widespread attention from researchers owing to their distinctive spectral properties including optical stability, long fluorescence lifetime, large Stokes shift, and sharp emission bands (Gao et al., 2018; Xiao et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2017). It has been reported that the β-diketonate configuration in tetracyclines are prone to coordinate vigorously with europium ions (Eu3+) to form steady tetracyclines–Eu3+ complexes (Han et al., 2020). The energy transfer that occurs from tetracyclines to Eu3+ can effectively achieve the sensitization of Eu3+ characteristic emission, which is also called the “antenna effect”, From this, an assay to detect tetracyclines can be realized based on the tetracyclines–Eu3+ complex (DeOliveira et al., 2007; Ibañez, 2008). Nevertheless, the vibrations of water molecules that have coordinated with Eu3+ can cause a significant quenching effect, as O-H is a highly efficient quencher of Ln3+ fluorescence, a property which depends on its vibronic coupling, which results in a weak emission of the tetracyclines-Eu3+ complex and a poor sensitivity for tetracyclines detection (Tan et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2020).

Currently, several fluorescence sensing platforms have been developed using nano-fluorescent materials as suitable ancillary ligands to protect the Eu3+ fluorescence from the quenching of water molecules, such as carbon dots (CDs) (Li et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020), silicon quantum dots (Zhang et al., 2021), semiconductor quantum dots (Han et al., 2020), and metal nanoclusters (Li et al., 2020b), thereby achieving the sensitive detection of tetracyclines. In particular, CDs that feature non-toxicity, simple fabrication, good water solubility, easy surface modification, and good biocompatibility, etc. function as excellent ancillary ligands to combine with Eu3+ (Lan et al., 2019; Lim et al., 2015). In addition, the characteristic fluorescence of CDs can also serve as a reference signal to achieve the ratiometric detection of tetracyclines. Compared to single-emission probes, ratiometric fluorescent probes on the basis of dual-emission signals can eliminate the interference caused by probe concentrations, instrument conditions and environmental factors, thereby achieving a more accurate and sensitive detection performance by self-calibrated fluorescence signals (Wang et al., 2020). Zheng et al. constructed a fluorescent system by coordinating graphene quantum dots with Eu3+ ions for the ratiometric and specific assay of three different tetracyclines, namely, OTC, TC and doxycycline (Li et al., 2018). Wang et al. also evolved a boron and nitride-doped carbon quantum dot and Eu3+ hybrid as a ratiometric fluorescent probe to determine the three tetracyclines (Yang et al., 2020). Despite the development of fluorescent strategies for the broad detection of tetracyclines, the specific identification of one or two tetracyclines remains highly difficult to achieve due to the large variety of tetracyclines and their highly similar structures. Therefore, the development of specific fluorescent probes for tetracyclines is still preferable.

Herein, we have developed a dual-response fluorescent probe by integrating nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and Eu3+ (N-CDs–Eu3+) for OTC and TC detection. As shown in Scheme 1 , before addition of OTC/TC, the N-CDs–Eu3+ system showed no characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+, only strong blue fluorescence emitting from N-CDs at 435 nm was observed. Upon inclusion of OTC/TC, the emission of N-CDs decreased due to the inner filter effect (IFE) as well as the static quenching effect (SQE) induced by OTC/TC. However, the red fluorescence of Eu3+ increased, which is ascribed to the energy transfer (ET) from OTC/TC to Eu3+, and the N-CDs can further enhance Eu3+ fluorescence by efficiently eliminating the quenching effect caused by H2O molecules. The probe presented double and reverse fluorescence response signals along with noticeable changes in the fluorescence color after inclusion of OTC/TC, hence realizing the ratiometric and visual detection of OTC and TC. Additionally, a smartphone-assisted portable sensing platform featuring low-cost and high ease of use was established for the rapid on-site determination of OTC.

The antibiotics oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline, doxycycline, metacycline, minocycline, penicillin, azithromycin, streptomycin, ciprofloxacin and norfloxacin were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), citric acid, adenine, glucose, ascorbic acid, glutathione, L-glutamic acid, L-cysteine, L-alanine, and L-leucine were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The metal salts were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Regents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The HEPES buffer (200 mM, pH 8.0) was prepared by titrating 200 mM HEPES aqueous solution with 3 M NaOH standard solution. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used to prepare the standard solutions used in the experiments. The apparatuses and characterization of N-CDs and N-CDs–Eu3+ were supplied in the supporting information.

The synthesis of N-CDs was performed by a hydrothermal process, as described in our previously reported work (Wu and Tong, 2020). Briefly, citric acid (1 mmol, 192 mg) and adenine (0.5 mmol, 67.5 mg) were added into 10 mL deionized water. After 10 min of sonication, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave, where the solution was left to react at 180 °C for 8 h, and a brownish-yellow N-CDs solution was obtained. Then, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and the obtained filtrate was further dialyzed with a membrane (1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the pure N-CDs solution was lyophilized to obtain N-CDs powder. N-CDs were also prepared with different molar ratios of citric acid and adenine (5:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:5) as precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of OTC and TC
ce:section.ce:para: Eu3+ (40 μM) was mixed with N-CDs (0.1 g·L−1) in HEPES buffer to construct the N-CDs–Eu3+ fluorescent probe. Then, a series of known concentrations of either OTC (0.1–45 μM) or TC (0.1–30 μM) were respectively added to the probe. After being incubated for 1 min, the fluorescence spectra of the probe were measured at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. To achieve the quantitative analysis of OTC/TC, the fluorescence ratio of 616 and 435 nm was calculated as a function of the OTC/TC concentration to establish a linear relationship. For the selectivity test, potential coexisting substances (50 μM) such as other antibiotics, amino acids, biomolecules, anions and cations in real samples were added to the probe instead of OTC/TC, and the test procedure was performed as described above. Additionally, anti-interference ability of the proposed method for OTC/TC detection was also examined. All the tests were performed in three parallel experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based portable device for OTC determination
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the N-CDs–Eu3+ fluorescent probe was prepared in a 32-well plate. Then, OTC with different concentrations was respectively added dropwise into the probe. Under a 365 nm UV lamp, a distinctive alteration in the fluorescence color of the probe system was observed by the naked eye. Fluorescence images were captured by the smartphone camera, and the image color was then converted into digital information (RGB values) by a color recognition application (ColorColl App) installed on the smartphone. The linear curve of the ratio of R and B values (R/B) versus OTC concentration was established. To ensure high detection accuracy, the probe solution was placed in a dark box and fluorescence images were obtained at night to eliminate interference from external ambient light. Three parallel experiments were performed to minimize the effects of any experimental errors.

The determination of OTC and TC in lake water, milk and honey samples was carried out using the fluorescent probe. Lake water samples were collected from Zilang Lake in Nantong, China and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane. Milk and honey obtained from the supermarket were pretreated before detection. The milk was diluted 10-fold with deionized water; Then, after adding 1 % trichloroacetic acid and undergoing 30 min of sonication, the precipitated protein in the milk was removed by centrifugation; Finally, the supernatant obtained was underwent purification by a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and the filtrate was collected for subsequent tests (Tan et al., 2013). The honey was diluted 10-fold and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Different concentrations of OTC (0, 1.0, 2.5, and 10 μM) and TC (0, 1.5, and 10 μM) standard solutions were added to the pretreated samples, respectively. The spiked samples were finally analyzed by the ratiometric detection method and smartphone-based portable device.

The morphology of the N-CDs was studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. As can be seen in Fig. 1A, B, the N-CDs present monodispersity and uniform distribution with an average diameter of 3.25 ± 0.20 nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the N-CDs (inset in Fig. 1A) reveals their crystallinity with a lattice space of 0.22 nm (Li et al., 2018; Wu and Tong, 2019). The elemental composition and chemical bonds at the surface of the N-CDs were investigated by recording the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1C, distinguishable binding energy peaks can be observed at 284.80, 400.12, and 530.56 eV in the XPS full spectra, and these are ascribed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Moreover, the weight percentage of the three elements was measured to be 53.81, 17.43, and 28.76 %. The high-resolution C1s spectrum shows four peaks at 284.08, 285.58, 286.95, and 288.08 eV stemming from the binding energy of C-C, C-N, C-O, and CN/CO, respectively (Fig. 1D) (Li et al., 2012b; Liu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2019). Three peaks at 394.48, 399.49, and 400.59 eV found in the N1s spectrum correspond to pyridinic type N, pyrrolic type N, and N-H, respectively (Fig. 1E) (Dong et al., 2013). The O1s spectrum can be fitted with two peaks at 530.05 and 531.13 eV, which actually originate from CO and C-OH/C-O-C, respectively (Fig. 1F) (Liu et al., 2012).

Additionally, the recorded Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum further illustrated the chemical composition of the N-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1G, the broad absorption band centered around 3310 and 2460 cm−1 are attributed to the O-H and COOH groups, respectively (Liu et al., 2012). The stretching vibrations of CO, C-O, C-O-C at 1700, 1400, and 1200 cm−1 confirmed that the oxygen-containing groups of CA were preserved in N-CDs in the hydrothermal reaction (Chen et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). Absorption bands at 3110, 1680, and 1600 cm−1 respectively originating from N-H, CN, and C-N stretching vibrations confirmed that Ade was doped in N-CDs (Wu and Tong, 2020). The FT-IR results demonstrated that the synthesized N-CDs contained various O and/or N-containing chemical groups, which was consistent with the obtained XPS spectrum results. The presence of hydrophilic compositions such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups endowed the N-CDs with excellent solubility.

The optical features of N-CDs were also studied via recording the absorption and fluorescence spectra. Two peaks at 285 and 335 nm can be observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1H, black line), which originate from the π-π* transitions of conjugated skeletons as well as the n-π* transitions of heteroatom-containing bonds, respectively (Wu et al., 2017). The recorded excitation spectra (λem = 418 nm) shows a peak at 335 nm (Fig. 1H, blue line) corresponding to the absorption peak. Meanwhile, strong emission spectra were collected from the N-CDs (Fig. 1H, red line), and blue fluorescence was observed (inset in Fig. 1H). The above results prove the truth that the luminescence of N-CDs may originate from the defect state emission on the basis of heteroatom-containing bonds (Bao et al., 2015). In addition, the N-CDs show an excitation-dependent emission characteristic, since large numbers of functional groups (including hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups) on the surface of N-CDs can produce various energy straps (Li et al., 2012a; Liu et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 1I, with the excitation wavelength of the N-CDs ranging from 280 to 390 nm, its fluorescence peak gradually shifted from 415 to 442 nm (inset in Fig. 1I).

For a more comprehensive investigation of the luminescence features of N-CDs, their fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield (QY) were measured. Two lifetime components of τ1 = 2.90 ns and τ2 = 9.94 ns present in the N-CDs fluorescence (Fig. S1), and the average lifetime (τave) is 4.37 ns according to Eq. S1. The QY of the N-CDs is measured to be 0.41, which is much higher than that of carbon dots prepared only with CA as precursor (Li et al., 2018). This is because N-CDs synthesized with citric acid and adenine as raw materials can introduce nitrogen to enrich surface functional groups and surface defects, thereby improving UV light absorption and the emission efficiency. Additionally, the stability of N-CDs under different pH values was explored. The N-CDs has weak emission intensity in weakly acidic aqueous solution, while the emission gradually enhances with increasing pH because of the deprotonation of its surface groups in alkaline solutions (Fig. S2). The pH-dependent luminescence also indicates that the surface traps serve as a trapping center for charge carriers, thereby generating correlative photoluminescence (Jia et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle and mechanism of the probe for OTC and TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of the N-CDs–Eu3+ hybrid and the principle for its ratiometric assay of OTC and TC is presented in Scheme 1. The N-CDs displayed strong blue fluorescence, and their combination with Eu3+ through surface functional groups did not affect their characteristic emission. Moreover, no Eu3+ fluorescence was observed in the N-CDs–Eu3+ hybrid. The reason for this phenomenon is that the relatively low absorption coefficient of Eu3+ and the quenching effect induced by the vibrations of H2O coordinated with Eu3+ (Tan and Chen, 2012). Upon addition of OTC/TC, ternary systems were formed in the N-CDs–Eu3+–OTC and N-CDs–Eu3+–TC complexes, which is attributed to the β-diketonate configuration in OTC/TC being combined with Eu3+ instead of water molecules (Yang et al., 2020). OTC/TC can efficiently absorb UV light and transfer energy to Eu3+ to sensitize its characteristic emission, which is also known as the “antenna effect”. Conversely, the blue fluorescence of the N-CDs significantly decreased because of the inner filter effect and static quenching effect by OTC/TC. Therefore, based on the double and opposite response signals, a ratiometric determination of OTC/TC can be achieved.

The response procedure of the probe to OTC/TC was confirmed by recording its fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime. As depicted in Fig. 2A, before inclusion of OTC/TC into the N-CDs–Eu3+ system, only blue emission from N-CDs was observed, and the Eu3+ ions showed a slight effect on their N-CD fluorescence. Upon inclusion of OTC/TC, there was a significant enhancement of the red fluorescence of Eu3+, which is ascribed to the hypersensitive transitions from 5D0 to 7F1 (592 nm) and 5D0 to 7F2 (616 nm), and this was accompanied by a significant reduction in the fluorescence of N-CDs. The fluorescence color of the probe also presented the apparent variation from blue to red (inset in Fig. 2A). In addition, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Eu3+ (λem = 616 nm) were measured. As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu3+ increased from 126.4 to 423.5 μs after addition of OTC with its concentration increasing from 5 to 20 μM. Similarly, the lifetime extension of the Eu3+ fluorescence upon addition of TC was also observed (Fig. S3A).

The enhancement mechanism of Eu3+ fluorescence, as promoted by OTC/TC, has been studied in details. UV–Vis adsorption spectra were collected as shown in Fig. 2B and S4, no noticeable alteration in the absorption band of N-CDs was observed upon inclusion of Eu3+. For both the binary complex of Eu3+–OTC and Eu3+–TC as well as the ternary complex of N-CDs–Eu3+–OTC and N-CDs–Eu3+–TC, a noticeable red-shift in the absorption peak from 360 to 390 nm and an enhancement in absorption intensity occurred as compared to free OTC/TC, indicating the coordination of OTC/TC with Eu3+ and N-CDs–Eu3+ hybrid. Then, the emission spectra of OTC/TC as well as binary system of the Eu3+–OTC and Eu3+–TC were recorded. As depicted in Fig. S5, the OTC and TC emission was almost completely quenched after addition of Eu3+, and this was accompanied by an improvement in the Eu3+ characteristic fluorescence, as the energy transfer process from OTC/TC to Eu3+ ions could occur effectively. As depicted in Fig. 3A, the first excited singlet state (S1) of OTC and TC is 27,777.8 cm−1 (360 nm), while the triplet state (T1) of OTC and TC as determined by the phosphorescence spectra is 19,200 cm−1 (541 nm) and 18,100 cm−1 (552 nm), respectively. According to Reinhoudt's empirical rule, efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) can occur in OTC and TC (Ghorai et al., 2013). In addition, the triplet state (T1) of OTC and TC are located above the 5D0 state (17,260 cm−1) of Eu3+, Dexter's sensitization mechanism is operative based on the energy transfer from OTC/TC to Eu3+, thereby improving Eu3+ emission and resulting in the quenching of OTC/TC fluorescence (Ghorai et al., 2013). Furthermore, OTC has higher sensitization efficiency for Eu3+ fluorescence compared to TC, since its T1 is higher.

It must be stated that the emission intensity of Eu3+ in the N-CDs–Eu3+–OTC ternary complex was 10 times that of the Eu3+–OTC binary complex. The reason for this phenomenon could be that Eu3+ combined with N-CDs instead of water molecules, thereby protecting Eu3+ from the vibrational quenching of OH. Although OTC/TC provided abundant coordination sites for Eu3+ ions, the limited OTC/TC ligands that were introduced caused the outcome that most sites of Eu3+ were occupied by water molecules. To illustrate the quenching effect of water molecules coordinated with Eu3+, the emission spectra and luminescence lifetimes of the Eu3+–OTC and N-CDs–Eu3+–OTC complexes in H2O and D2O buffer were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3B, compared to H2O buffer, the emission intensity of Eu3+ in both binary and ternary systems increased in D2O buffer. In addition, the number of coordinated “H2O” (nw) was determined by taking advantage of the equation (Eq. (1)) developed by Horrocks and Supkowski (Supkowski and Horrocks, 2002):

where τH 2 O and τD2O represent the fluorescence lifetimes of Eu3+ in aqueous and D2O buffer, respectively. The nw in the Eu3+–OTC system was 9.3, which decreased to 3.8 in the N-CDs–Eu3+–OTC system. The significant reduction of nw demonstrated that the Eu3+ ions were well shielded from the H2O in the ternary system. Consequently, the quenching effect from O-H was reduced, resulting in the increased emission and lifetime of Eu3+ (Table S1). Therefore, the enhancement mechanism of Eu3+ fluorescence was not only attributed to the fact that OTC/TC coordinated with Eu3+ accompanied by energy transfer, but also that the N-CDs served as an ancillary ligand to effectively protect Eu3+ from the quenching effect of water molecules.

To explore the quenching mechanism of N-CDs fluorescence, absorption spectra of OTC/TC, excitation spectra of N-CDs, and both emission spectra and fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-CDs upon inclusion of OTC/TC were recorded. A large overlap presented between the absorption spectra of OTC/TC and the excitation spectra of N-CDs (Fig. S6), suggesting that the inner filter effect from OTC/TC resulted in the quenching of N-CDs emission (Zhai et al., 2014). Furthermore, TC exhibited a stronger quenching effect on N-CDs fluorescence as it showed a stronger absorbance, which increased the inner filter effect of TC on the N-CDs. In addition, quenching experiments were also performed, a decrease in N-CDs fluorescence was observed while gradually increasing the OTC/TC concentration (Fig. S7 A, C), and the fluorescence quenching process was analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (2)) (Zhai et al., 2014):

0
/F is linearly related with Cq, and Ksv is 1.785 × 104 and 2.686 × 104 M−1 in the presence of OTC and TC, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime measurement of N-CDs was performed. As exhibited in Fig. 2D and S3 B, C, upon inclusion of different concentrations of OTC/TC into the N-CDs and N-CDs-Eu3+ system, the lifetime decay curves of fluorescence at 435 nm remained relatively stable, indicating the static quenching effect of N-CDs fluorescence induced by OTC/TC. Moreover, the τ0 of the N-CDs is 4.37 ns, and the K q for OTC and TC is calculated to be 4.08 × 1012 and 6.15 × 1012 M−1·s−1, respectively, which are much higher than that of dynamic quenching mechanism (1.0 × 1010 M−1·s−1) (Zhai et al., 2014). These results illustrate that the inner filter effect and static quenching effect are the reason for OTC/TC-induced fluorescence quenching of N-CDs.

Various conditions, including the synthesis of N-CDs, concentrations of the N-CDs and Eu3+, pH, and incubation time were optimized to improve the performance of OTC and TC detection. Firstly, the N-CDs synthesis condition was studied. The molar ratio between citric acid and adenine precursors (MCA:MAde) has a significant impact on fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs–Eu3+ hybrid. With decreasing MCA:MAde, the N-CDs fluorescence is enhanced, owing to the increased content of elemental nitrogen (Fig. S8A). Upon inclusion of OTC/TC into the probe, the Eu3+ fluorescence increases while that of N-CDs is quenched simultaneously (Fig. S8 B, C). When the ratio of MCA:MAde is 1:5, the Eu3+ fluorescence is particularly weak and the F616/F435 reaches a minimum (Fig. S8D). Therefore, considering both the emission intensity of the probe and its sensitive response to OTC/TC, an MCA:MAde of 1:2 was chosen. Then, the concentrations of N-CDs and Eu3+ were optimized as important factors. As shown in Fig. S9 A–D, the Eu3+ emission of the probe increases significantly after addition of OTC/TC when the N-CDs concentration exceed 0.1 g·L−1, while F616/F435 gradually decreases with an increasing N-CDs concentration, on this basis, a concentration of N-CDs at 0.1 g·L−1 was chosen. As shown in Fig. S10 A–C, the Eu3+ concentration shows a significant effect on Eu3+ fluorescence and has a slight effect on that of N-CDs, respectively. Moreover, with Eu3+ in a concentration of 40 μM, the F616/F435 of the N-CDs–Eu3+ system reaches a maximum upon addition of OTC/TC (Fig. S10D). Hence, a Eu3+ concentration of 40 μM was chosen. Additionally, the effect of a pH ranging from 6.0 to 10.0 on the N-CDs–Eu3+ system for OTC and TC detection was examined. As depicted in Fig. S11A–C, after inclusion of OTC/TC, the Eu3+ emission from the probe enhances gradually with increasing pH. This is because OTC/TC are prone to deprotonation and are negatively charged in alkaline solutions, which is conducive to the electrostatic combination between OTC/TC and Eu3+ (Ravikumar and Panneerselvam, 2019). When the pH is 8.0, a maximum value of F616/F435 is obtained (Fig. S11D), indicating that a pH of 8.0 is the optimal condition. Finally, the incubation time was investigated. Upon inclusion of OTC/TC, F616/F435 has almost reached essential equilibrium at 1 min and remains stable in 60 min (Fig. S12), revealing that the coordination between N-CDs–Eu3+ and OTC/TC is completed within 1 min, and a highly stable complex is formed. Together, an MCA:MAde of 2:1, pH of 8.0, N-CDs concentration of 0.1 g·L−1, Eu3+ concentration of 40 μM, and equilibration time of 1 min were the optimal results of the probe for OTC and TC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric determination of OTC and TC
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the above-described optimal conditions, the response performances of this probe to OTC/TC were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 A, D, before inclusion of OTC/TC, strong N-CDs fluorescence with no noticeable Eu3+ emission was observed. Upon addition of OTC/TC and with their increasing concentration, the Eu3+ fluorescence increased while the CIP fluorescence decreased. The double and reverse response signals allow for the development of a ratiometric assay for OTC/TC detection. As depicted in Fig. 4 B, E, F
616 /F
435 gradually increased with increasing OTC/TC concentration. For OTC, an excellent linear correlation of F
616 /F
435
versus the OTC concentration (COTC) in the range of 0.1–45 μM was obtained. The linear equation is F
616 /F
435
= 0.05089COTC - 0.03239 with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9975. The limit of detection (LOD) of OTC calculated on the basis of the equation LOD = 3δ/k is 0.017 μM, which is below the acceptable level (100 ng/mL) for OTC in milk proposed by the Food Safety and Standard Authority of India (FSSAI) (Birader et al., 2021). For TC, F
616 /F
435 increased linearly as the TC concentration increased from 0.1 to 30 μM. The corresponding working curve is F
616 /F
435
= 0.0363CTC + 0.00291 (R2 = 0.9905). The LOD for TC was calculated to be 0.041 μM, which is far below the allowable residue limit in milk (225 nM) regulated by the Food and Drug Administration of the United States and European Union (Jia et al., 2019). In addition, compared to the previously reported fluorescent probes, the wide quantitative range and sensitivity of the newly developed approach are comparable or more advantageous (Table S1).

Furthermore, under a UV lamp of 365 nm, successive apparent alterations were observed in the fluorescence color of the N-CDs–Eu3+ probe, initially from blue to purple, and then to red with increasing OTC/TC concentrations (inset in Fig. 4B, E). This result was confirmed by the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates computed based on the fluorescence spectra of the probe after addition of OTC/TC (Fig. 4C, F). These results make it possible to effectively realize the fast and visual semi-quantitative determination of OTC and TC with the naked eye according to the obtained fluorescence color variation. Herein, a fluorescence sensing platform with visualization, a wide detection range as well as high sensitivity for OTC and TC detection was developed based on the N-CDs-Eu3+ system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and anti-interference of the probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The specific response and anti-interference ability of analytical methods are the prerequisites to achieving target detection in complex samples. Therefore, in the presence of OTC, TC, other antibiotics (including chlortetracycline, doxycycline, metacycline, minocycline, penicillin, azithromycin, streptomycin, ciprofloxacin, and norfloxacin), representative amino acids, glucose, ascorbic acid, and common inorganic ions, the fluorescence response of the probe was examined. Only after the inclusion of OTC and TC was Eu3+ fluorescence significantly enhanced (Fig. 5 A, B), and this was accompanied by a decrease in N-CDs fluorescence (inset in Fig. 5 A, B), no representative signal change of F
616 /F
435 was observed when the probe was incubated with 50 μM of other antibiotics and interfering substances. In addition, only OTC and TC triggered the fluorescence color alteration of the probe, which was easily distinguishable with the naked eye, hence realizing the fast recognition of OTC and TC through visual analysis. Moreover, compared to the fluorescent methods reported recently for tetracyclines detection, our probe showed a superior degree of specificity for OTC and TC detection (Table S2). Anti-interference analysis was also performed in the coexistence of the above interferents and OTC/TC. The presence of coexisting foreign substances caused a negligible effect on the selective response of the probe to OTC and TC (Figs. 5 C, D and S13). Furthermore, in the presence of coexisting foreign substances, an identical fluorescence color change was observed compared to that upon addition of only OTC or TC alone (inset in Figs. 5 C, D and S13). All results confirm that this probe can determine OTC and TC with excellent specificity and anti-interference, thereby establishing a promising, novel method for OTC and TC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted portable device for OTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under UV lamp at a wavelength of 365 nm, a successive alteration in the fluorescence color of the probe upon inclusion of OTC at different concentrations was easily discernible with the naked eye. However, the accurate determination of OTC content is impossible while using only the naked eye, which cannot distinguish slight changes in fluorescence color, nor reliably quantify the color. Therefore, a simple and low-cost sensing platform was developed by integrating a smartphone application, ColorColl App, as a fluorescence color reader to achieve the on-site and real-time quantitative detection of OTC (Shen et al., 2022a; Shen et al., 2022b; Shen et al., 2022c). As shown in Fig. 6 , after the probe was prepared in 24-well plates, it displayed clear blue fluorescence when irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp. After inclusion of OTC, a gradual variation in the fluorescence color of the probe was noticed. Then, color images were captured by the camera of a smartphone, and the ColorColl App converted the fluorescence color into RGB values. As depicted in Fig. S14, the percentage of R increased, and this accompanied by a decrease in B with COTC increasing. Furthermore, the relationship of R and B ratio (R/B) versus COTC was examined, and a good linear curve of R/B versus COTC (R2 = 0.9930) was obtained, while the LOD was 0.15 μM. In addition, the slope of the linear equation of R/B versus COTC was very close to that of F616/F435 versus COTC measured with a fluorescence spectrometer, indicating that the instrument-free sensing platform created by the integration of a smartphone APP can be applied for the rapid and economical quantitative detection of OTC, thereby making it possible to perform the OTC assay under on-site and outdoor conditions. Several methods reported by Shen et al. that are based on the smartphone sensing platform for on-site assay were found to achieve satisfactory results.

To validate the practicability and reliability, the determination of OTC and TC in lake water, milk, and honey samples was performed with the proposed method. As shown in Tables 1 and S3, no OTC and TC residues were determined in the pretreated samples, therefore, the recovery investigation were carried out through the standard addition method. The OTC and TC recoveries obtained by the ratiometric fluorescence assay ranged from 97.2 to 106.1 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) of less than 2.8 %. Moreover, the intra-day and inter-day precision of ratiometric method was investigated by determining OTC in lake and milk samples. The RSD of the intra-day and inter-day precision were measured to be less than 5.7 % and 6.4 %, respectively (Table S4). These results demonstrate the satisfactory reliability and accuracy of the ratiometric assay. Additionally, the OTC content in samples was determined using the smartphone-assisted portable analysis device. As shown in Fig. 7 , a distinguishable variation in the fluorescence color of the probe was noticed after inclusion of OTC-spiked samples, and the OTC concentration determined by the smartphone-assisted method was consistent with that determined by the ratiometric method. Overall, satisfactory results were obtained for the detection of OTC and TC in real samples, thereby confirming the reliability and applicability of the proposed method.

In conclusion, a N-CDs–Eu3+ dual-response system was successfully developed to function as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the sensitive and visual determination of OTC and TC. The N-CDs were prepared from citric acid and adenine as raw materials by a one-step hydrothermal process, and the N-CDs–Eu3+ probe was fabricated simply by mixing N-CDs with Eu3+. The fluorescent probe only emitted strong blue fluorescence from the N-CDs. The inclusion of OTC/TC can enhance Eu3+ fluorescence attributing to the energy transfer from OTC/TC to Eu3+, and the incorporation of N-CDs can further improve the Eu3+ fluorescence by efficiently eliminating the quenching effect caused by H2O molecule, while blue emission from the N-CDs was quenched significantly because of inner filter effect and static quenching effect. The fluorescence response of the probe to OTC/TC was rapid and complete within 1 min. More importantly, based on the double and reverse response signals, the ratiometric detection of OTC/TC was realized with excellent specificity and anti-interference. Moreover, the probe displayed a clear fluorescence color change, thereby allowing for visual differentiation and the semi-quantitative assay of OTC/TC with the naked eye. Additionally, a smartphone-assisted readout analysis method that features with low cost, portability, and high ease of use has been developed for the rapid and on-site determination of OTC, which is of paramount importance for food safety and environmental monitoring.

Huifang Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Designed and conceived the project, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Yubing Chen: Performed the experiments, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Mengqi Xu: Investigation, Performed the experiments, Visualization. Yuwei Ling: Validation, Software. Shiying Ju: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yanfeng Tang: Funding acquisition, Designed and conceived the project, Writing - review & editing. Changlun Tong: Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing. All authors participated in the discussion and commented on the results.
